content:
Case 2:
Initial presentation:
- Age: 24
- Sex: female
- Chief complaint: New skin changes on the back and extremities
- Vital signs: BP 120/75 mmHg, HR 116/min, T° 36.8 °C, respiratory rate 16/min.
Setting: Outpatient/Day Unit (Initial Presentation)
Capabilities: You are evaluating a clinically stable patient with new symptoms in an ambulatory or day-unit setting. You can perform a focused history and examination, obtain routine blood panels, and arrange standard non-invasive imaging as first-line tests. Invasive tissue sampling and specialized analyses usually require additional coordination and are not available in this round.
Provide either a request for a single information item or solve.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "focused physical examination (full skin exam description of lesions, distribution, mucosal involvement; lymph node exam; hepatosplenomegaly)"}}

response.request: focused physical examination (full skin exam description of lesions, distribution, mucosal involvement; lymph node exam; hepatosplenomegaly)

content:
History Physical:
On June 4, 2025, the patient presented with new skin changes, first noticed by her partner on the back and now also on the extremities. She reported intermittent headaches and mild nausea, as well as chronic joint pain and stiffness. Otherwise, she was in good general condition, afebrile, and showed no signs of skin or mucosal GvHD. A marked psychological stress reaction was noted.
Current medication: pantoprazole 40 mg 1-0-0, vitamin D 20,000 IE 1×/week, calcium 500 mg 1-0-0, posaconazole 300 mg 1×/d, ruxolitinib 10 mg 1-0-1, valaciclovir 500 mg 1-0-1, co-trimoxazole 960 mg orally once daily, three times per week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday), steroid cream p.r.n.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "complete blood count with differential (including absolute neutrophil count, eosinophils, platelets)"}}

response.request: complete blood count with differential (including absolute neutrophil count, eosinophils, platelets)

content:
Cbc:
CBC: Hb 14.2 g/dL, WBC 6.8 ×10⁹/L, Plt 240 ×10⁹/L; MCV 89 fL, MCH 30 pg; differential: neutrophils 58%, lymphocytes 32%, monocytes 7%, eosinophils 2%, basophils 1%.

less likely without fever but immunosuppressed)", "Small-vessel vasculitis or connective tissue disease flare (given arthralgias/stiffness)", "Leukemia/lymphoma relapse with skin involvement (leukemia cutis) or post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (less likely with normal CBC but cannot exclude)"], "treatment_plan": [{"action": "Obtain 12-lead ECG and arrange urgent transthoracic echocardiography; repeat troponin/NT-proBNP and assess for heart-failure/myocarditis symptoms\u2014consider same-day hospital admission if any dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, hypotension, or reduced EF", "intention": "diagnostic", "priority": 1}, {"action": "Dermatology evaluation with full-body skin assessment and plan for skin biopsy with histology \u00b1 direct immunofluorescence at next available opportunity (to distinguish cGvHD vs drug eruption vs vasculitis vs malignancy)", "intention": "diagnostic", "priority": 2}, {"action": "Supportive rash management while awaiting work-up: continue/optimize topical corticosteroid to affected areas and emollients; review and avoid new topical products; consider non-sedating antihistamine if pruritic; do not empirically stop prophylaxis/immunosuppression without transplant-team review unless rash becomes severe (blistering, mucosal involvement, systemic symptoms)", "intention": "supportive", "priority": 3}]}}}

solve.working_diagnosis: New generalized rash/skin changes in an immunosuppressed likely post–allogeneic HSCT patient on ruxolitinib: most consistent with a non-severe inflammatory rash (drug eruption vs early cutaneous chronic GvHD), with concerning concurrent evidence of cardiac strain/injury (tachycardia with markedly elevated NT-proBNP and mildly elevated troponin) requiring urgent cardiac evaluation
differentials: Cutaneous chronic graft-versus-host disease (early/atypical presentation despite no obvious classic features on initial exam)
Medication-related exanthem (including antimicrobial-associated or other delayed hypersensitivity eruption)
Viral exanthem/reactivation (e.g., HHV6/EBV/CMV, VZV; less likely without fever but immunosuppressed)
Small-vessel vasculitis or connective tissue disease flare (given arthralgias/stiffness)
Leukemia/lymphoma relapse with skin involvement (leukemia cutis) or post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (less likely with normal CBC but cannot exclude)

treatment_plan.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG and arrange urgent transthoracic echocardiography; repeat troponin/NT-proBNP and assess for heart-failure/myocarditis symptoms—consider same-day hospital admission if any dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, hypotension, or reduced EF

Dermatology evaluation with full-body skin assessment and plan for skin biopsy with histology ± direct immunofluorescence at next available opportunity (to distinguish cGvHD vs drug eruption vs vasculitis vs malignancy)

Supportive rash management while awaiting work-up: continue/optimize topical corticosteroid to affected areas and emollients; review and avoid new topical products; consider non-sedating antihistamine if pruritic; do not empirically stop prophylaxis/immunosuppression without transplant-team review unless rash becomes severe (blistering, mucosal involvement, systemic symptoms)

{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "skin_biopsy"}}

Skin Biopsy:
Skin biopsy (2025-06-07): The dermis and subcutis are diffusely infiltrated by atypical hematopoietic cells in a perivascular and interstitial distribution. The cells show irregular nuclear contours, vesicular chromatin, and occasional nucleoli. Mitotic figures are frequent. Immunohistochemistry: CD34-, CD33+, MPO+, Lysozyme and CD68+, CD117+, negative for CD20-, CD19-, CD3-. Molecular pathology: NPM1 mutation present in cutaneous infiltration.
{"response": {"action": "request", "request": "bone_marrow_biopsy (aspirate/biopsy morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics/FISH, and molecular including NPM1 MRD if available)"}}

response.request: bone_marrow_biopsy (aspirate/biopsy morphology, flow cytometry, cytogenetics/FISH, and molecular including NPM1 MRD if available)

content:
Bone Marrow:
June 5, 2025: Morphology: Normocellular bone marrow with age-appropriate cellularity. Trilineage hematopoiesis present with some dysmorphic maturation in erythroid, myeloid, and megakaryocytic lineages, consistent with reactive changes after alloSCT. No excess blasts or abnormal infiltrates were observed.

content:
Pet Ct:
FDG-PET/CT (2025-06-07): Revealed multiple FDG-avid cutaneous lesions (SUVmax 6.2) and intra-abdominal lesions adjacent to the left ureter (SUVmax 8.1), accompanied by several mildly FDG-avid mediastinal lymph nodes (SUVmax 4.5).

myeloid markers positive)", "Reactive/infectious dermal infiltrate (histology/IHC and molecular confirm malignant myeloid infiltrate)", "Systemic marrow relapse of AML below morphologic detection threshold (needs flow/MRD monitoring despite negative morphology)"], "treatment_plan": [{"action": "Confirm extent and clonality: obtain marrow flow cytometry and molecular MRD (NPM1 RT-qPCR) from bone marrow and peripheral blood; if not available, consider venetoclax + hypomethylating agent (azacitidine/decitabine) or intensive re-induction depending on prior therapy/fitness, with plan to bridge to cellular therapy (donor lymphocyte infusion and/or 2nd alloSCT) if response achieved", "intention": "therapeutic", "priority": 1}, {"action": "Local control of symptomatic/bulky sites: involved-site radiotherapy to cutaneous or ureter-adjacent lesion if pain/obstruction risk; continue antimicrobial prophylaxis and add tumor lysis and infectious monitoring with therapy", "intention": "therapeutic", "priority": 2}, {"action": "Supportive care during salvage: TLS prophylaxis (hydration \u00b1 allopurinol), transfusion support as needed, antimicrobial prophylaxis per neutropenia risk, and prompt management of febrile episodes", "intention": "supportive", "priority": 3}]}}}

solve.working_diagnosis: Isolated extramedullary relapse of NPM1-mutated acute myeloid leukemia after allogeneic stem cell transplantation, presenting as myeloid sarcoma/leukemia cutis with additional FDG-avid intra-abdominal extramedullary lesions, with no morphologic marrow relapse on current biopsy
differentials: Chronic GVHD skin involvement (excluded by biopsy showing MPO+ myeloid blasts and NPM1 mutation)
Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder/lymphoma (IHC negative for B/T markers; myeloid markers positive)
Reactive/infectious dermal infiltrate (histology/IHC and molecular confirm malignant myeloid infiltrate)
Systemic marrow relapse of AML below morphologic detection threshold (needs flow/MRD monitoring despite negative morphology)

treatment_plan.action: Confirm extent and clonality: obtain marrow flow cytometry and molecular MRD (NPM1 RT-qPCR) from bone marrow and peripheral blood; baseline labs (CBC, CMP, coagulation) for treatment readiness

Initiate AML-directed salvage therapy for extramedullary relapse post-alloSCT (prefer clinical trial); if not available, consider venetoclax + hypomethylating agent (azacitidine/decitabine) or intensive re-induction depending on prior therapy/fitness, with plan to bridge to cellular therapy (donor lymphocyte infusion and/or 2nd alloSCT) if response achieved
Local control of symptomatic/bulky sites: involved-site radiotherapy to cutaneous or ureter-adjacent lesion if pain/obstruction risk; urology consult if hydronephrosis develops

Adjust immunosuppression to enhance graft-versus-leukemia effect (in coordination with transplant team): taper/stop ruxolitinib if feasible while monitoring for GVHD flare; continue antimicrobial prophylaxis and add tumor lysis and infectious monitoring with therapy
Supportive care during salvage: TLS prophylaxis (hydration ± allopurinol), transfusion support as needed, antimicrobial prophylaxis per neutropenia risk, and prompt management of febrile episodes

content: {"response": {"action": "request", "request": "treatment_history"}}

Treatment History:
Diagnosis: 2024-02-21, AML with NPM1 mutation (WHO 2022). ELN 2022 risk: favorable.
Initial immunophenotype (February 21, 2024): CD34^low^ blasts expressing CD33, CD14, CD64, CD117, MPO, and CD13, negative for HLA-DR, CD7, CD56, and TdT.
Induction (Cycle 1): “7+3” (cytarabine 200 mg/m² for 7 days (2,352 mg total) + daunorubicin 60 mg/m² for 3 days (303 mg total)) plus gemtuzumab ozogamicin 5 mg on days 1, 4, and 7 (15 mg total). Complications: Neutropenic fever and pneumonia, otherwise well tolerated.
Consolidation (March–July 2024): Four cycles of high-dose cytarabine (3 g/m² BID for 3 days = 30,240 mg total per cycle). Response: Complete remission (CR). MRD negativity in bone marrow (flow cytometry and NPM1 PCR) achieved after the first consolidation cycle.
September 14, 2024: Patient noted palpable swellings in the chest. Gynecologic evaluation showed bead-like lymph nodes on ultrasound. Biopsy confirmed AML infiltration. Repeated bone marrow biopsy demonstrated ongoing CR but increased NPM1 levels (ratio 1.42). PET-CT was not performed. Donor search initiated.
Salvage therapy (September 20, 2024): Venetoclax 400 mg days 1–28 plus azacitidine 75 mg/m² days 1–7. September 28, 2024: Hospitalization due to neutropenic fever. Meropenem 1 g TID was initiated for 7 days (21 g total). Posaconazole 300 mg/day was started on day 13 of cycle 1, and venetoclax was reduced to 70 mg for the last 14 days (total dose 6,180 mg). Patient was discharged on October 8, 2024. After one cycle, extramedullary lesions were no longer palpable. Neutrophils recovered on October 18, 2024. PET-CT confirmed no further delineable lesions.
Second cycle (November 2, 2024): Venetoclax 70 mg days 1–14 (due to comedication with posaconazole 300 mg), achieving complete remission.
Allogeneic SCT (December 7, 2024): Haploidentical sibling donor; conditioning with fludarabine 30 mg/m² for 5 days, treosulfan 14 g/m² for 3 days, and thiotepa 5 mg/kg twice on day -2. GVHD prophylaxis: Post-transplant cyclophosphamide (50 mg/kg on days +3 and +4), mycophenolate mofetil (MMF), and tacrolimus.
Complications: Febrile episodes with suspected central venous catheter infection. Empiric antibiotic therapy was initiated with piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g TID for 3 days, later escalated to meropenem 1 g i.v. TID for 13 days, and vancomycin 1 g BID (later increased to 1.5 g-0-1.25 g for 5 days) for Gram-positive coverage. Hemolysis was suspected (haptoglobin <10 mg/dL, schistocytes 6‰ on peripheral smear, and LDH 324 U/L, bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL) in the setting of ABO-incompatible transplantation. The patient received rituximab 375 mg/m² i.v. weekly × 4 doses (total 2,520 mg).
January 10, 2025: Complete remission (CR); 100% donor chimerism; no MRD detectable in bone marrow.
March 28, 2025 (day +100): PET-CT showed no FDG-avid lesions. Day +107: Patient developed skin GvHD grade III°, steroid-refractory (Days 1–7: prednisolone 120 mg daily, tapered by 10 mg every 3 days) but responsive to ruxolitinib 10 mg BID. GvHD overlapped into mild to moderate chronic GvHD, controlled with ruxolitinib 10 mg BID.

Given prior response to venetoclax+azacitidine and transplant status, consider re-treatment with venetoclax + HMA (with strict posaconazole interaction dose-adjustment) OR alternative relapse regimen (e.g., FLAG-IDA/CLAG-M if fit, or targeted agent if new actionable mutation). Aim for rapid cytoreduction and bridge to immunologic consolidation (donor lymphocyte infusion if feasible) and/or second alloSCT if durable remission cannot be maintained", "intention": "therapeutic", "priority": 1}, {"action": "Immunomodulation to enhance graft-versus-leukemia while balancing GVHD: taper/stop ruxolitinib as tolerated and minimize other immunosuppression in coordination with transplant team; monitor closely for GVHD flare and treat promptly if occurs", "intention": "therapeutic", "priority": 2}, {"action": "Local control for threatening/symptomatic extramedullary sites: involved-site radiotherapy to bulky/painful cutaneous lesions or ureter-adjacent mass; urology evaluation and stenting if obstruction/hydronephrosis develops", "intention": "therapeutic", "priority": 3}, {"action": "Infection/supportive care plan tailored to anticipated neutropenia: antimicrobial prophylaxis per institutional post-transplant/venetoclax protocols; tumor lysis monitoring and prophylaxis as indicated; transfusion support and growth factor per regimen; frequent CBC/chemistry and early evaluation of fever", "intention": "supportive", "priority": 3}]}}}

solve.working_diagnosis: Second isolated extramedullary relapse of NPM1-mutated AML after haploidentical alloSCT (myeloid sarcoma/leukemia cutis with additional PET-avid intra-abdominal/mediastinal sites) with no morphologic marrow relapse; represents post-transplant AML relapse with high risk of impending systemic relapse despite favorable-risk biology at diagnosis
differentials: Systemic (marrow) relapse below morphologic threshold (requires flow cytometry and NPM1 RT-qPCR MRD from marrow and blood)
Post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder/lymphoma involving skin/nodes (less likely given MPO+/CD33+ myeloid phenotype and NPM1 mutation in lesion)
Cutaneous GVHD or drug exanthem mimicking rash (excluded by biopsy showing malignant myeloid infiltrate)

treatment_plan.action: Complete relapse staging and establish measurable disease: peripheral blood and bone marrow NPM1 RT-qPCR (MRD) plus multiparameter flow cytometry; review prior/lesion cytogenetics/NGS and obtain current lesion NGS if not already done; baseline echo/ECG and cardio-oncology consult given prior anthracycline exposure and current NT-proBNP/troponin elevation

Start systemic salvage therapy for post-alloSCT extramedullary AML relapse (tumor board/clinical trial preferred). Given prior response to venetoclax+azacitidine and transplant status, consider re-treatment with venetoclax + HMA (with strict posaconazole interaction dose-adjustment) OR alternative relapse regimen (e.g., FLAG-IDA/CLAG-M if fit, or targeted agent if new actionable mutation). Aim for rapid cytoreduction and bridge to immunologic consolidation (donor lymphocyte infusion if feasible) and/or second alloSCT if durable remission cannot be maintained

Immunomodulation to enhance graft-versus-leukemia while balancing GVHD: taper/stop ruxolitinib as tolerated and minimize other immunosuppression in coordination with transplant team; monitor closely for GVHD flare and treat promptly if occurs

Local control for threatening/symptomatic extramedullary sites: involved-site radiotherapy to bulky/painful cutaneous lesions or ureter-adjacent mass; urology evaluation and stenting if obstruction/hydronephrosis develops

Infection/supportive care plan tailored to anticipated neutropenia: antimicrobial prophylaxis per institutional post-transplant/venetoclax protocols; tumor lysis monitoring and prophylaxis as indicated; transfusion support and growth factor per regimen; frequent CBC/chemistry and early evaluation of fever
